candidate_problems.text: Is the conjectured mechanism actually necessary, or only common? In other words: for finite agents under uncertainty, is some in-group/out-group boundary formation mathematically or institutionally unavoidable for coordination, or can there exist social arrangements that preserve discrimination in action without crystallizing into identity-like moralized groups?

What determines whether anti-tribal selection remains a thin, functional trust heuristic versus escalating into a thick status identity centered on moral purity? The unresolved tension is the transition condition: which feedback loops, incentives, and enforcement structures cause a coordination filter ('reliable cooperator') to become a prestige hierarchy ('the properly enlightened')?

How can one distinguish legitimate norm-based exclusion required for cooperation from merely rebranded tribalism? The open question is whether there is a principled criterion separating necessary social filtration from identity-boundary formation, or whether every exclusion regime eventually collapses into tribal structure at a higher level of abstraction.
